preFix.title: Fitting a quadrant moulding to a floor edge
preFix.subtitle: Cover the expansion gap at a floating floor with a pinned quarter-round strip
preFix.excerpt: How to measure, mitre, and pin quadrant moulding at the junction of a floating floor and the skirting board: cutting internal and external mitres cleanly, pinning to the skirting rather than the floor, and finishing with flexible filler.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

content.text: requires a 10 to 12 mm expansion gap at every wall and obstacle.

covers this gap and gives a finished edge to the floor. The one rule that distinguishes a good installation from a poor one: pin the quadrant to the skirting board only, never through the floor. A pin driven into the floor locks it and prevents expansion, causing the boards to buckle.

Quadrant moulding in the species or finish to match the floor. Panel pins, 25 mm, and a pin hammer. A tenon saw and a 45-degree mitre box, or a hand mitre saw. Tape measure, combination square, and carpenter's pencil. Fine-surface filler for the pin holes and a sanding block for finishing before painting.
Step 1, measure each run and mark the mitres

Measure each wall run individually, from corner to corner. At internal corners, both pieces are mitred at 45 degrees. At external corners (for example around a chimney breast), both pieces mitre outward at 45 degrees. Mark the long point and short point of each mitre on the moulding with a pencil before cutting.
Step 2, cut and fit the lengths

Cut the mitre using a tenon saw in a mitre box, or a hand mitre saw set to 45 degrees. Test each length in position before pinning: the flat face of the moulding presses against the skirting board and the curved face rests on the floor. Drive 25 mm panel pins at a slight downward angle into the skirting board at 300 mm intervals, keeping the pin position within the upper half of the quadrant so it enters the skirting and not the floor.
Step 3, fill pin holes and finish

Punch each pin head just below the surface using a pin punch. Fill the recesses with fine-surface filler, allow to dry, then sand flush with 120-grit on the sanding block before painting the moulding and skirting together. If the moulding is a pre-finished wood species, leave the pin holes unfilled or use a matching coloured filler wax.
What it looks like when it is right

The moulding sits tightly against the skirting board along its full length. The curved face rests on the floor without gaps. Corner mitres close without opening when the moulding is pressed in from both sides. The floor boards beneath move freely when pressed downward and released; if they do not spring back, a pin has gone through the floor.

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
